ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'milk tea' class (FOODON_03301103); no specific entry for low-fat hazelnut milk tea exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific hazelnut milk tea code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for hazelnut milk tea. None assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: One large takeaway cup of low-fat hazelnut milk tea, approximately 500 ml, as served at KOI Thé Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Milk Tea, prepared with low-fat milk and flavoured syrup — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (black tea, low-fat milk 1%, hazelnut syrup) and NCC Food and Nutrient Database category anchor for flavoured milk tea beverages
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central; NCC Food and Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for low-fat hazelnut milk tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA Branded Foods. Values estimated by combining: (1) USDA FDC SR Legacy — black tea brewed (FDC ID 171917), (2) USDA FDC SR Legacy — milk, low-fat, fluid, 1% milkfat (FDC ID 171265), and (3) hazelnut syrup contribution modelled on USDA Branded Food hazelnut coffee syrup entries. Approximate blend ratio: 40% brewed tea, 45% low-fat milk, 15% hazelnut syrup by volume. Per-100g values derived from this weighted blend. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of milk-based tea beverages and sweetened milk drinks — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened low-fat milk (GI ~30–34) and flavoured syrup contribution raising composite GI to approximately 41
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for low-fat hazelnut milk tea. Composite GI estimated as follows: low-fat milk GI ~32 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Am J Clin Nutr); hazelnut syrup is predominantly sucrose-based, GI ~65. Weighted blend (45% milk, 15% syrup, 40% tea with negligible GI contribution) yields composite GI ≈ 41, classified as low. Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (41 × 49 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 20.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~140 mg per 500 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single large serving contributes approximately 140 mg sodium, which is modest relative to the 2000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; however, frequent consumption of sweetened milk teas should be monitored as part of overall dietary sodium and caloric intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content from hazelnut syrup (~46 g sugar per 500 ml serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Each large serving delivers approximately 46 g of free sugars primarily from hazelnut flavouring syrup, which can cause a meaningful postprandial glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request reduced or no syrup and consider smaller serving sizes or sugar-free alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat due to low-fat milk base (~2 g saturated fat per 500 ml serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The use of low-fat milk limits saturated fat exposure to approximately 2 g per large serving, which is within acceptable limits for cardiovascular health; the primary concern is the high free-sugar load from hazelnut syrup, which may contribute to hypertriglyceridaemia with habitual consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Low Fat Hazelnut Milk Tea (Large) is a Taiwanese-style flavoured milk tea beverage sold at KOI Thé, a popular Taiwanese tea chain with outlets across Singapore. The drink is prepared by combining brewed black tea with low-fat milk and hazelnut-flavoured syrup, then shaken or stirred and served chilled in a large cup of approximately 500 ml. The flavour profile is sweet and nutty, with a creamy mouthfeel derived from the low-fat milk and a pronounced hazelnut aroma from the syrup. The texture is smooth and liquid, lighter than full-fat milk tea variants. Nutritionally, the beverage provides approximately 260 kcal per large serving, with 8 g protein, 3 g fat, and 49 g carbohydrate, of which approximately 46 g is sugar from the hazelnut syrup. The low-fat milk base contributes calcium (~290 mg per serving), vitamin B12, riboflavin, and potassium. The glycemic index is estimated at 41 (low GI category), though the high free-sugar load results in a moderate glycemic load of approximately 20 per serving. Clinically, the beverage is of moderate concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to the high free-sugar content from hazelnut syrup; requesting reduced syrup or a sugar-free variant is advisable. Saturated fat is low due to the low-fat milk formulation, making it a better cardiovascular choice than full-fat milk tea. Sodium content is modest. The drink is vegetarian and halal-suitable. It contains milk and hazelnut allergens.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for low-fat hazelnut milk tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods, or ICMR-NIN. Values are estimated by component blending using USDA SR Legacy data for brewed black tea, low-fat milk (1%), and hazelnut syrup. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for low-fat milk and sucrose-based syrups. Medium confidence is appropriate given the estimation methodology.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data (black tea FDC ID 171917; low-fat milk 1% FDC ID 171265) and hazelnut syrup modelled on USDA Branded Food hazelnut coffee syrup entries. No exact branded product entry was available. 2. Serving size of 500 g is an approximation for a large (L) cup as served at KOI Thé Singapore; actual volume may vary by outlet. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for brewed tea or low-fat milk at the level of precision required for this blend estimate. 4. vitamin_e_mg is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used for this estimation. 5. selenium_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in low-fat milk and tea-based beverages per USDA FDC data. 7. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible in liquid tea-milk beverages with no solid particulates per USDA FDC data — naturally zero for this beverage type. 8. GI set to 41 (low category) based on analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database; glycemic load per 500 g serving = 20.1. 9. Food is served at KOI Thé - Funan, Singapore (field survey data listing, food_id GD001356). 10. Halal status assumed based on KOI Thé Singapore's general halal certification; individual outlet certification should be verified. 11. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03301103 (milk tea) used as closest parent; FoodEx2 A0CKP (tea-based beverages) used as closest parent; no SNOMED CT code assigned.

Iteration 2: Verified aliases_by_language consistency — confirmed all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in flat aliases. Confirmed GI > 0 (set to 41). Confirmed ontology match_type values use only allowed strings (exact, closest_parent, none). Confirmed Type 2 Diabetes human_condition_name. All hard gates and Q1–Q8 pass.
